source.title: BSEE Accident Investigation Report - Structural Damage / Crane Incident

context.region: Gulf of Mexico - West Cameron Block 33

event.top_event: Wire rope sling failure and struck-by injury
actions_taken: First aid provided to injured person on derrick barge
Motor Vessel Hannah Ray used for evacuation when helicopter crew unavailable
Injured person transferred to Cameron dock and awaiting ambulance at 10:49 pm
BSEE onsite follow-up inspection conducted on August 14, 2023
Physical inspection of abrasive cutter and wire rope sling assembly completed
Job Safety and Environmental Analysis (JSA) documents reviewed
event.summary: On August 10, 2023, at approximately 5:15 pm, during crane operations to remove a water abrasive cutter from a 48-inch caisson at WC-33 #1 platform, a 2.5-ton wire rope sling in the lifting arrangement parted and struck an Offshore Technical Services (OTS) employee on the head and neck. The incident occurred when the abrasive cutter hung up in the caisson simultaneously with a wave surge, introducing dynamic loading that exceeded the safe working limit of the sling. The injured person sustained a forehead laceration requiring 16 stitches and a C6 vertebrae fracture. Root causes included inadequate Job Safety and Environmental Analysis (JSA) that did not address dynamic loading from adverse sea conditions (5-7 foot sea state), failure to call an all-stop after the initial hang-up, and personnel positioned near the suspended load contrary to JSA requirements.
recommendations: Address possible hazards with adverse sea conditions and dynamic loading in JSAs for crane operations
Include maximum lift weight and sling limitations in JSAs
Call all-stop to address new threats when suspended loads hang up
Enforce requirement for personnel to stay well clear of suspended loads
Install handrails around open caissons during operations
Ensure personnel wear required safety harnesses during crane operations

hazards.name: Adverse sea conditions and wave surge
Wire rope sling assembly under dynamic loading

threats.name: Dynamic loading from wave surge during lift
Wire rope sling exceeding safe working limit
Lack of handrails around open caisson

consequences.name: Loss of work time injury (>3 days)

controls.name: Job Safety and Environmental Analysis (JSA)
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to identify hazards and mitigation measures

supporting_text: In the JSA, the total weight to be lifted was not mentioned nor were the limitations of the slings described.
The JSA did not have written threats of the dynamic loading of waves during the lift or the lift hanging up in the 48-inch caisson.
In the JSA, the total weight of the lift was not written.

controls.name: Wire rope sling assembly with safe working limits
controls.barrier_role: Engineering control to limit load capacity

supporting_text: The vertical safe working limit is 2.5 tons for all five slings in the sling assembly below the tool lift beam.
The maximum lift for retrieving the abrasive cutter was 2.5 tons, which was the safe working limit of the wire rope slings.
The abrasive cutter hung up simultaneously to a wave surge introducing an undetermined amount of dynamic loading parting the two-foot single-leg sling.

controls.name: All-stop procedure for new threats during lifting
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to halt operations when hazards emerge

supporting_text: The JSA listed adverse wind and sea conditions as a Potential Hazard, and the recommendation to eliminate or reduce potential hazards was to 'Call All Stop If Necessary'.
During the lift, the abrasive cutter hung up in the caisson. An all-stop was not called to address any new threats.
An all-stop was not called to address any new threats after the abrasive cutter hung up in the 48-inch caisson the first time.

controls.name: Personnel positioning away from suspended loads
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to prevent struck-by incidents

supporting_text: The JSA required personnel to 'stay well clear of suspended loads'.
Apache did not address personnel standing near the suspended load at the time of the incident.
The JSA required personnel to 'stay well clear of suspended loads', personnel were allowed to stand near the suspended load.
In one of the witness's statements, the IP was witnessed looking down the 48-inch caisson when the wire rope sling parted.

controls.name: Handrails around open caisson
controls.barrier_role: Engineering control to prevent falls into open caisson

supporting_text: Investigators noted there were no handrails around the open 48-inch caisson on the deck of the platform in the picture directly after the incident.
Handrails were not installed around the open 48-inch caisson during cutting operations on the platform deck.
The 48-inch caisson opening is approximately one foot above the walking deck of the platform as seen in submitted pictures.

controls.name: Safety harness requirement for personnel near open caisson
controls.barrier_role: Personal protective equipment to prevent falls

supporting_text: A safety meeting document dated August 10, 2023, from Manson Construction with both OTS and Manson personnel attending lists the following hazard: 'open holes'.
The personnel were not wearing safety harnesses as required in the safety meeting.

controls.name: Barricading and flagging of open holes
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to prevent access to open caisson

supporting_text: A safety meeting document dated August 10, 2023, from Manson Construction with both OTS and Manson personnel attending lists the following hazard: 'open holes'. The recommendation to eliminate or reduce potential hazards was to 'flag barricade off open holes'.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
